intro.snapshot_description: One of the most formula-rich chapters in NEET physics, Gravitation demands mastery of three interlinked pillars: Newton's law of gravitation and variation of g, gravitational potential energy and escape velocity, and orbital mechanics with Kepler's laws. The chapter sits at the boundary of classical mechanics and celestial physics — every major formula involves G or g, and the majority of NEET traps stem from sign errors in potential energy (U = −GMm/r is negative) or confusing the direction of change of orbital speed versus time period with altitude. Students who commit the five key expressions (g, g′ at height, g′ at depth, v_escape, v_orbital) and the three Kepler laws to instant recall will reliably score 3 marks here.

expected_questions.description: Gravitation consistently contributes 3 questions per NEET paper; occasionally 4 when a Kepler assertion-reason is included. Standard split: 1 numerical on g variation or satellite orbital speed, 1 on escape velocity or gravitational PE, 1 on Kepler's laws or geostationary satellite.

time_required.description: Theory + formula derivations 3 hrs; g variation (altitude, depth, latitude, rotation) 1.5 hrs; gravitational PE, escape velocity and orbital velocity 2 hrs; Kepler's laws + geostationary satellite 1.5 hrs; MCQ bank drill 1.5 hrs.

difficulty_level.description: Formulas are compact but the sign of gravitational PE (always negative) and the inverse-square vs linear depth variation of g trip up most students. Once these two conceptual hurdles are cleared the chapter becomes highly predictable and formula-driven.

intro.most_asked_style: Numerical MCQ: find g at height h or depth d; find escape velocity or binding energy; find orbital speed or time period at given height; compare speeds of two satellites; Kepler T² ∝ r³ ratio problems.
intro.biggest_trap: Applying the same formula for g above AND below the Earth's surface. Above surface: g′ = g(1 − 2h/R) for h << R or g/(1+h/R)² exactly. Below surface: g′ = g(1 − d/R). The depth formula is LINEAR; the height formula is INVERSE-SQUARE. Using the height approximation for depth (or vice versa) is the single most common error in NEET Gravitation problems.
intro.fast_win: Memorise these five results and you answer 60% of Gravitation MCQs instantly: (1) g = GM/R² = 9.8 m/s², (2) v_escape = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s, (3) v_orbital(surface) = √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s, (4) v_escape = √2 × v_orbital at same radius, (5) geostationary height ≈ 36,000 km, T = 24 h.
intro.revision_friendly: Yes. All major formulas fit on two flashcard sides. The sign of PE (always negative, more negative = bound more tightly) and the two g-variation profiles (linear below, inverse-square above) are the only conceptual points that need active reinforcement.

subtopics.subtitle: Four major blocks: Newton's law of gravitation and variation of g with altitude, depth, latitude, and Earth's rotation; gravitational potential and potential energy including escape velocity; orbital motion, satellite mechanics, and geostationary orbits; and Kepler's three laws of planetary motion with applications.
subtopics.revision_tip: Before any Gravitation numerical, classify the problem: (1) Is it about g variation → identify altitude vs depth and use correct formula. (2) Is it about energy → remember U = −GMm/r is negative and total energy E = −GMm/2r for circular orbit. (3) Is it about orbital motion → v_o = √(GM/r), T² ∝ r³. (4) Is it about Kepler → identify which law and whether angular momentum conservation is needed. This four-step triage eliminates formula confusion.

topics.topic_name: Newton's Law of Gravitation and Variation of g
topics.topic_description: Establishes the universal law F = Gm₁m₂/r² (G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻²) and derives surface gravity g = GM/R² = 9.8 m/s². Then derives four variation formulae: with altitude g′ = g/(1+h/R)² ≈ g(1−2h/R) for h<<R; with depth g′ = g(1−d/R), linear decrease, g=0 at centre; with latitude g′ = g − Rω²cos²λ, poles > equator; effect of Earth's rotation reducing apparent weight. Key inequality: g is maximum at the surface.

subtopics.name: Universal Law of Gravitation and G
subtopics.description: F = Gm₁m₂/r² where G = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻². G is universal constant — does not vary with medium, temperature, or position. Force is along the line joining the two masses, attractive in nature, central force, obeys principle of superposition. g = GM/R² at Earth's surface. Relationship between G and g: g = GM/R², so M = gR²/G. g on Moon ≈ g/6 because while M_moon is 80 times less, R_moon is 4 times less, net g_moon ≈ 1.62 m/s². Weight W = mg varies with location; mass is invariant.

subtopics.name: Variation of g with Altitude and Depth
subtopics.description: Above surface (altitude h): g′ = GM/(R+h)² = g × R²/(R+h)². For h << R: g′ ≈ g(1 − 2h/R). Rate of decrease = 2g/R per unit height — decreases faster with height than with depth for same shift. Below surface (depth d): g′ = g(1 − d/R). Linear decrease; g = 0 at centre (d=R). Maximum g is at Earth's surface, not at centre or above. For same fractional change: 2% decrease in g requires h = R/100 above surface but d = R/100 below surface halves the fractional decrease — height formula gives twice the change of depth formula for same distance.

subtopics.name: Variation of g with Latitude and Earth's Rotation
subtopics.description: Due to Earth's rotation: g′ = g − Rω²cos²λ where λ is latitude. At equator (λ=0): max reduction, g′ = g − Rω². At poles (λ=90°): no reduction, g_pole = g. Hence g_pole > g_equator. Two combined factors: (1) Earth is oblate — poles are closer to centre so g_pole > g_equator due to smaller radius. (2) Centrifugal pseudo-force at equator further reduces apparent g. Combined: g_pole − g_equator ≈ 0.052 m/s²≈ 0.5% difference. Rockets launched eastward at equator to exploit Earth's rotational velocity ≈ 465 m/s as free boost.

topics.topic_name: Gravitational Potential Energy and Escape Velocity
topics.topic_description: Defines gravitational potential V = −GM/r (scalar, always negative) and gravitational PE U = −GMm/r (always negative, zero at infinity). Establishes that more negative PE means more tightly bound. Derives escape velocity v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s — independent of mass of projectile. Shows binding energy = GMm/(2r) = ½mv_e² at surface. Key property: total mechanical energy E = KE + PE = −GMm/(2r) for circular orbit (negative = bound).

subtopics.name: Gravitational Potential and Potential Energy
subtopics.description: Gravitational potential V = −GM/r (J/kg); always negative; V=0 at r=∞. Gravitational PE of mass m at distance r: U = mV = −GMm/r (J); always negative. At Earth's surface: U_surface = −GMm/R. Change in PE to move mass from surface to height h: ΔU = GMm(1/R − 1/(R+h)) = mgh/(1+h/R) ≈ mgh for h<<R. PE of system of 4 particles at corners of square of side a each of mass m: U = −4Gm²/a − 2Gm²(a√2) = −Gm²/a(4 + √2). Inside a uniform shell: gravitational field = 0, so potential = constant = −GM/R throughout interior. Sign reminder: U more negative = lower potential = more bound = harder to escape.

subtopics.name: Escape Velocity and Binding Energy
subtopics.description: Escape velocity v_e: minimum speed for object to escape gravitational field from surface. Energy condition: KE = −PE → ½mv_e² = GMm/R → v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR). At Earth's surface: v_e = √(2 × 9.8 × 6.4×10⁶) ≈ 11.2 km/s. CRITICAL: v_e is independent of mass of projectile (m cancels). Does not depend on direction of projection. Relationship with orbital speed at surface: v_e = √2 × v_orbital. Escape from height h: v_e(h) = √(2GM/(R+h)) = v_e × √(R/(R+h)) — decreases with height. Binding energy = energy required to move to infinity = GMm/(2R) at surface for satellite in circular orbit. From infinity falling to surface: body gains speed = v_e.

topics.topic_description: Derives orbital velocity v_o = √(GM/r) = √(gR²/r) and shows it DECREASES as height increases. Derives time period T = 2π√(r³/GM) and shows it INCREASES as height increases. For surface orbit: v_o ≈ 7.9 km/s, T ≈ 84 min. Geostationary satellite: T = 24 h, h ≈ 36,000 km, equatorial orbit, west-to-east. Total energy of satellite in circular orbit: E = −GMm/(2r); KE = GMm/(2r); PE = −GMm/r; |PE| = 2 × KE always. Binding energy = GMm/(2r). Weightlessness in orbit is due to free fall, not absence of gravity.

subtopics.name: Orbital Velocity and Time Period
subtopics.description: Orbital velocity: gravitational force provides centripetal force → GMm/r² = mv²/r → v_o = √(GM/r). Rewriting using g = GM/R²: v_o = R√(g/r) = √(gR²/r). At surface (r=R): v_o = √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s. As r increases: v_o DECREASES (inverse √r). At height h: v_o = √(gR²/(R+h)). Time period: T = 2πr/v_o = 2π√(r³/GM). As r increases: T INCREASES (proportional to r^(3/2)). Near earth surface: T ≈ 2π√(R³/GM) = 2π√(R/g) ≈ 84 min = 1 h 24 min. Geostationary orbit: T = 24 h, orbit radius r ≈ 42,250 km, height h ≈ 35,880 km ≈ 36,000 km. Geostationary characteristics: equatorial orbit, west-to-east direction, appears stationary from Earth, used for communications (INSAT group).

subtopics.name: Energy of Satellite and Weightlessness
subtopics.description: For circular orbit of radius r: KE = ½mv² = GMm/(2r); PE = −GMm/r; Total E = KE + PE = −GMm/(2r) (negative = bound). Therefore |PE| = 2 × KE (virial theorem for gravity). Binding energy = −E_total = GMm/(2r). To move from orbit r₁ to r₂ (r₂>r₁): additional KE needed = ½GMm(1/r₁ − 1/r₂). If orbit radius decreases by 1%: speed increases by 0.5% (v ∝ 1/√r). Weightlessness: g inside satellite ≠ 0; astronaut is in constant free fall — normal force from floor = 0. Objects in satellite are in same free-fall orbit, so relative acceleration = 0. Rockets launched eastward at equator: Earth's rotational speed ≈ 465 m/s adds to launch speed.

topics.topic_description: States and applies all three Kepler laws: (1) Law of orbits — planets move in ellipses with Sun at one focus; (2) Law of areas — radius vector sweeps equal areas in equal times (dA/dt = constant = L/2m, consequence of angular momentum conservation); (3) Law of periods — T² ∝ a³ where a is semi-major axis, equivalently T² ∝ r³ for circular orbit. For circular orbit: T²/r³ = 4π²/GM = constant for all planets around same star. From Kepler 2: planet is fastest at perihelion (closest) and slowest at aphelion (farthest). Kinetic energy is highest at perihelion, lowest at aphelion.

subtopics.name: Kepler's First and Second Laws
subtopics.description: First law (Law of Orbits): Every planet revolves around the Sun in an elliptical orbit with the Sun at one of the two foci of the ellipse. Circle is a special case of ellipse. Second law (Law of Areas): The line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time. Consequence of angular momentum conservation: L = mvr = constant for central force. Since dA/dt = L/(2m) = constant, and L = mvr: when r is small (perihelion, closest point), v must be large to keep L constant → planet moves fastest. When r is large (aphelion, farthest point), v is small → planet moves slowest. At perihelion: KE maximum, PE minimum (U more negative), total E constant. At aphelion: KE minimum, PE maximum (U less negative). For ellipse SB perpendicular to major axis: K_C > K_B > K_A where C is perihelion, A is aphelion.

subtopics.name: Kepler's Third Law and Applications
subtopics.description: Third law (Law of Periods): The square of the time period of revolution of a planet is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its elliptical orbit. T² ∝ a³, or T² = (4π²/GM) × r³ for circular orbit. T₁²/T₂² = r₁³/r₂³ for any two planets around the same Sun. Application: ratio of distances d₁/d₂ known → T₁/T₂ = (d₁/d₂)^(3/2). Example: if d₁/d₂ = 1.38, then T₁/T₂ = 1.38^(3/2). Frequency n = 1/T, so n₁²d₁³ = n₂²d₂³ = constant (Kepler 3rd in frequency form). Geostationary orbit derived from T² ∝ r³: known T_geo = 24 h, T_nearearth ≈ 1.4 h → r_geo/R = (24/1.4)^(2/3) ≈ 6.6 × R. Kepler 3rd also validates Newton's inverse-square law: combining v_o = √(GM/r) and T = 2πr/v gives T² = 4π²r³/GM ∝ r³.

topics.topic_name: Newton's Law of Gravitation and Variation of g
topics.topic_description: The foundational framework: universal gravitation formula, derivation of surface g, and the four distinct contexts where g varies (altitude, depth, latitude, rotation).
topics.notes_condensed_description: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻². g = GM/R² = 9.8 m/s². Altitude (exact): g′ = g/(1+h/R)²; approx: g′ ≈ g(1−2h/R). Depth: g′ = g(1−d/R); g=0 at centre. Latitude: g′ = g − Rω²cos²λ; g_pole > g_equator. At equator both oblate shape and centrifugal reduction act. g_max at Earth's surface. Rate: altitude decreases g at twice the rate depth does for same small distance.
topics.how_to_revise: Write the two g-variation formulae side by side: altitude g′ = g(1−2h/R) vs depth g′ = g(1−d/R). Note the factor of 2 in altitude. Then solve 3 problems where question gives same numerical value for h and d and ask for which reduction is larger — altitude always wins by factor 2. This locks in the distinction permanently.

expected_questions.description: 1 direct numerical per paper on g at height or depth. Occasionally both appear in the same assertion-reason pair. Very reliable 1-mark topic.

time_required.description: 45 min derivation of g and two variation formulas; 45 min solved examples; 30 min MCQ drill on altitude vs depth traps.

difficulty.description: Formulas are simple; the only difficulty is remembering which formula applies to height vs depth, and the factor of 2 in the altitude approximation.

topics.scoring_focus: Three key numerical results to memorise: g′ at h=R is g/4; g′ at d=R/2 is g/2; g′ at centre is 0. These appear as direct targets in NEET options.
topics.high_risk_area: Using altitude formula g(1−2h/R) for depth, or depth formula g(1−d/R) for altitude. Both look similar. Check: altitude has coefficient 2, depth does not.
topics.best_practice_style: Create a single diagram: Earth cross-section with a vertical axis. Mark altitude formula above surface, depth formula below. Label the factor of 2 difference in red. Review this diagram before each exam.
topics.priority_rule: High. Nearly guaranteed 1 mark per paper. Cover within first 2 hours of chapter study. Foundational for all subsequent satellite and PE calculations.

topics.topic_name: Gravitational Potential Energy and Escape Velocity
topics.topic_description: The energy framework of gravitation: negative PE with zero at infinity, escape velocity derivation, and the binding energy concept for satellites.
topics.notes_condensed_description: U = −GMm/r; always negative; U=0 at infinity. More negative → more bound. v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s. Independent of mass m and direction of launch. v_e = √2 × v_orbital at same r. Binding energy at surface = GMm/(2R) = ½mv_e². From infinity to surface: body gains KE = GMm/R = ½mv_e². ΔPE for small height = mgh/(1+h/R) ≈ mgh.
topics.how_to_revise: Prove from scratch: (1) Set total energy = 0 for escape (barely escapes). (2) ½mv² + (−GMm/R) = 0 → v = √(2GM/R). Note m cancels. This cancellation is the source of the mass-independence fact — derive it once and the fact is unforgettable.

expected_questions.description: 1 MCQ most years on escape velocity calculation or comparison between planets; sometimes a binding energy or PE change calculation.

time_required.description: 45 min PE sign convention and formula; 45 min escape velocity derivation and comparison problems; 30 min binding energy MCQs.

difficulty.description: The mathematics is straightforward but the sign of PE (-) confuses students into errors. The key conceptual hurdle is understanding that more negative PE does not mean less energy stored — it means more bound.

topics.scoring_focus: v_e ≈ 11.2 km/s and v_e = √2 × v_o are the two highest-frequency facts. Memorise both along with v_e² = 2gR for quick numerical checks.
topics.high_risk_area: Claiming escape velocity depends on mass of the projectile. It does not — m cancels in the energy equation. NEET exploits this by offering options with the mass of the object included.
topics.best_practice_style: Solve the comparison: If planet radius is 4R and escape velocity is 3v_e of Earth, find its mass. Set up v_e(planet)² = 2GM_planet/R_planet; 9v_e² = 2GM_planet/(4R) → M_planet = 18v_e²R/G = 18gR³/G ≈ 216M_Earth. This style recurs in NEET exactly.
topics.priority_rule: High. 1-2 marks per paper. Allocate 20% of chapter time. Master escape velocity before tackling orbital motion.

topics.topic_description: The mechanics of circular satellite orbits: orbital speed, time period, energy relationships, geostationary orbit, and weigh­tlessness.
topics.notes_condensed_description: v_o = √(GM/r) = R√(g/r) — decreases with r. T = 2π√(r³/GM) — increases with r. At surface: v_o = √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s; T ≈ 84 min. Geostationary: T=24h, h≈36000km, equatorial westward-to-eastward. Energy: KE=GMm/2r; PE=−GMm/r; E_total=−GMm/2r. |PE|=2KE. Binding energy = GMm/2r. 1% decrease in r → 0.5% increase in v_o. Weightlessness: free-fall orbit, not zero gravity.
topics.how_to_revise: Draw a table with three rows: (1) quantity, (2) formula, (3) direction of change as r increases. Fill in: v_o, T, KE, PE, E_total, binding energy. Seeing all quantities together with their r-dependence in one table eliminates confusion about which increases and which decreases with altitude.

expected_questions.description: 1 MCQ per paper — most commonly comparison of orbital speeds or time periods of two satellites; occasionally geostationary period or height; assertion-reason on weightlessness in space.

time_required.description: 1 hr orbital velocity and time period derivations; 45 min energy analysis of orbit; 45 min geostationary + weightlessness MCQs.

difficulty.description: The trap is the counterintuitive direction of changes: orbital speed decreases as height increases, but time period increases. Students expect higher orbit = faster. The energy table clarifies this immediately.

topics.scoring_focus: The fact that v_e = √2 × v_o for the same radius is a direct NEET MCQ answer. Also: energy to escape from orbit = KE of satellite (because binding energy = KE for circular orbit).
topics.high_risk_area: Claiming orbital velocity increases with height. It decreases (v ∝ 1/√r). Time period increases (T ∝ r^(3/2)). NEET tests this direction-of-change understanding directly.
topics.best_practice_style: Master the result: KE of satellite in orbit = binding energy = GMm/2r. So energy to escape from orbit = KE — the satellite only needs one more KE worth of energy. This is a recurring NEET MCQ calculation.
topics.priority_rule: High. 1-2 reliable marks. Covers directly the most numerically tested area of the chapter. Allocate 25% of chapter time here.

topics.topic_description: The three empirical laws of planetary orbits: ellipse geometry, equal-areas angular-momentum conserved, and T² ∝ r³ period-distance law.
topics.notes_condensed_description: K1: Ellipse with Sun at focus. K2: Equal areas in equal time; dA/dt = L/2m = const; fastest at perihelion (closest), slowest at aphelion. K3: T² ∝ a³; T₁²/T₂² = r₁³/r₂³. Frequency form: n₁²d₁³ = n₂²d₂³. At perihelion: KE max, PE min. At aphelion: KE min, PE max. Total energy constant throughout. Kepler 3rd derivation using circular orbits: T² = 4π²r³/GM.
topics.how_to_revise: Write all three laws in one sentence each, then identify the physics principle behind each: K1 = orbital shape (ellipse, Sun at focus), K2 = angular momentum conservation (L = constant), K3 = T² ∝ r³ (Newton's inverse-square law consequence). Then solve 2 ratio problems using K3: given two orbital radii, find ratio of periods. This covers the direct NEET question type.

expected_questions.description: 1 MCQ every 1-2 papers: usually a Kepler 3rd law calculation (ratio of periods for ratio of distances) or an assertion about speed at perihelion vs aphelion.

time_required.description: 30 min three laws with physics behind each; 30 min areal velocity and angular momentum connection; 30 min T² ∝ r³ ratio problems.

difficulty.description: Kepler's laws are conceptually clear once you link K2 to angular momentum and K3 to Newton's law. The only difficulty is the ratio algebra in K3 problems — straightforward with practice.

topics.scoring_focus: T₁/T₂ = (r₁/r₂)^(3/2) — this formula directly solves 80% of Kepler MCQs. Memorise it. Also: at perihelion v is max → KE is max → PE is min (most negative). At aphelion the reverse.
topics.high_risk_area: Claiming the orbital speed is constant in an elliptical orbit — it is NOT (only constant in a circle). Kepler's 2nd law assertion-reason traps exploit this: the wrong reason states 'orbital speed is constant' when the correct reason is 'angular momentum is conserved'.
topics.best_practice_style: Solve: Two planets at distances d and 2d from Sun. Frequency ratio? n₁²d³ = n₂²(2d)³ → n₁²/n₂² = 8 → n₁/n₂ = 2√2. This is the exact form from NEET past papers.
topics.priority_rule: Medium. 1 reliable mark per paper. Allocate 15% of chapter time. Cover last after mastering other three topics.

topics.subtopic_name: g Variation Formula — Same Formula Cannot Be Used Above AND Below Surface

mistakes.label: Using altitude approximation g(1−2h/R) for a depth problem:
mistakes.description: The formula g′ = g(1−2h/R) is valid ONLY for small heights above the surface. For depth d below the surface, the correct formula is g′ = g(1−d/R) — linear decrease with NO factor of 2. Using the altitude formula for a depth question overestimates the reduction of g by a factor of 2 and gives a wrong answer. NEET always provides the wrong altitude-formula answer as a distractor for depth problems.

mistakes.label: Claiming g is maximum at the centre of Earth or in outer space:
mistakes.description: g is maximum at Earth's surface. Below the surface g decreases linearly to zero at the centre. Above the surface g decreases as inverse-square. At the centre g = 0, not maximum. NEET assertion-reason questions directly test this: 'A body becomes weightless at the centre of Earth' is a TRUE assertion with the reason that g=0 there — NOT that gravity does not exist.

topics.example: Body at depth d = R/2 below surface. Correct: g′ = g(1 − 1/2) = g/2. Using wrong altitude formula: g′ ≈ g(1 − 2×(R/2)/R) = g(1−1) = 0 — gives zero, which is the answer for the centre only. NEET provides g/2 and 0 as options; altitude-formula error leads to the wrong option 0.
topics.trap_framing: NEET asks: 'A body is taken to a depth R/2 below Earth's surface. What is the new value of g?' and provides options g/2, 0, g/4, 2g. Students who memorise 'altitude formula' without reading 'depth' select 0 — wrong.

trap_question.stem: The radius of Earth is R. At what depth below the surface is the acceleration due to gravity equal to half its surface value?
trap_question.trick: Depth formula: g′ = g(1−d/R) = g/2 → 1−d/R = 1/2 → d = R/2. Option A correct. Using altitude formula g(1−2h/R) = g/2 → h = R/4 — this gives Option B, which is the distractor for students who confuse altitude and depth formulas.

topics.quick_rule: Formula has factor 2 → ALTITUDE. Formula has no factor 2 → DEPTH. g′ = g(1−2h/R) altitude; g′ = g(1−d/R) depth. Maximum g at surface; g=0 at centre.

topics.subtopic_name: Escape Velocity Is Independent of Projectile Mass

mistakes.label: Claiming escape velocity depends on mass m of the body being projected:
mistakes.description: From energy conservation: ½mv_e² = GMm/R → v_e = √(2GM/R). The mass m cancels completely. Escape velocity is the same for a hydrogen atom and a spacecraft launched from the same surface. NEET provides planet escape velocity questions where students factor in body mass and get an answer that includes m — this is always wrong.

mistakes.label: Confusing escape velocity with orbital velocity and using v_e = v_o instead of v_e = √2 v_o:
mistakes.description: At the same radius r: v_e = √(2GM/r) and v_o = √(GM/r), so v_e = √2 × v_o ≈ 1.414 × v_o. A satellite in orbit needs √2 times its orbital speed to escape — an additional factor of √2, not a factor of 2.

topics.example: Escape velocity on a planet where radius = 4R_E and escape velocity = 3v_e_E. What is its mass? v_e_planet² = 2GM_planet/(4R) = 9v_e_E² = 9 × 2gR → GM_planet = 9 × 2gR × 2R = 36gR² → M_planet = 36gR²/G = 36M_E (since gR² = GM_E). Correct: mass = 36M_E. NEET provides options including values divided by m — always wrong.
topics.trap_framing: NEET states: 'Escape velocity of a body of 1 kg mass on a planet is 100 m/s. Gravitational potential energy of the body at that planet is?' Gravitational PE = −GMm/R = −½mv_e² = −½×1×10000 = −5000 J. The mass DOES appear here in PE — trap is misapplying where m appears (in PE, not in v_e).

trap_question.stem: The escape velocity from Earth is 11.2 km/s. What will be the escape velocity from a planet having twice the radius and the same mean density as Earth?
trap_question.trick: v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2G × (4/3πR³ρ)/R) = R√(8πGρ/3). So v_e ∝ R when density is constant. Planet has R_p = 2R_E → v_e_planet = 2 × 11.2 = 22.4 km/s. Option A correct. Common mistake: not recognising v_e ∝ R with same density, instead using M unchanged and getting wrong answer.

topics.quick_rule: Escape velocity: m cancels → INDEPENDENT of projectile mass. v_e = √(2gR) at surface. v_e = √2 × v_orbital at same radius. v_e ∝ R when density is constant (M ∝ R³ then v_e ∝ R).

topics.subtopic_name: Orbital Velocity Decreases with Height — Time Period Increases

mistakes.label: Claiming orbital velocity increases as satellite height increases:
mistakes.description: v_o = √(GM/r). As r increases, v_o decreases — inversely proportional to √r. This is counterintuitive because a higher orbit seems to require 'more effort and speed'. In reality, the gravitational pull is weaker at higher orbits, so less centripetal force is needed, achieved by a slower speed. A 1% decrease in orbital radius causes a 0.5% increase in speed.

mistakes.label: Claiming time period decreases when satellite moves to a higher orbit:
mistakes.description: T = 2π√(r³/GM) ∝ r^(3/2). As r increases, T increases. Higher orbit = slower speed AND larger circumference → much longer period. Geostationary orbit at 36,000 km has T = 24 h. Near-Earth orbit at ~400 km has T ≈ 90 min. The period increases dramatically with altitude.

topics.example: Satellite A at orbital radius R, satellite B at 4R. Speed ratio: v_A/v_B = √(r_B/r_A) = √(4R/R) = 2. Period ratio: T_A/T_B = (r_A/r_B)^(3/2) = (1/4)^(3/2) = 1/8. So T_B = 8T_A. B at higher orbit is SLOWER (half the speed) and has period 8 times longer. Students who invert the speed ratio get T ratio inverted too — double error.
topics.trap_framing: NEET: 'A satellite is moving around Earth with speed V in a circular orbit of radius r. If orbit radius is decreased by 1%, its speed will...' Answer: increase by 0.5% (v ∝ r^{−1/2}; −1/2 × (−1%) = +0.5%). Trap options include 'decrease by 0.5%' (wrong sign).

trap_question.stem: Two satellites of same mass orbit Earth at heights h₁ and h₂ where h₂ > h₁. Which of the following is correct?
options: Orbital speed: v₁ > v₂ and Time period: T₁ > T₂
Orbital speed: v₁ < v₂ and Time period: T₁ < T₂
Orbital speed: v₁ > v₂ and Time period: T₁ < T₂
Orbital speed: v₁ < v₂ and Time period: T₁ > T₂
trap_question.trick: h₂ > h₁ → r₂ > r₁. v_o ∝ 1/√r → v₁ > v₂ (satellite at lower height is faster). T ∝ r^(3/2) → T₁ < T₂ (lower orbit has shorter period). Option C is correct: v₁ > v₂ AND T₁ < T₂. Both change in the 'same direction' — lower orbit → faster speed AND shorter period. Students who confuse one direction get option A or D.

topics.quick_rule: Higher orbit → LOWER orbital speed (v ∝ 1/√r). Higher orbit → LONGER period (T ∝ r^(3/2)). Both trends go the SAME way: lower orbit = faster speed AND shorter period. Remember: GPS satellites at 20,000 km orbit slower than ISS at 400 km.

topics.subtopic_name: g at Earth's Centre Is Zero — Maximum g Is at Surface

mistakes.label: Claiming g is maximum at Earth's centre because everything is 'pulled from all sides':
mistakes.description: g = 0 at the centre, NOT maximum. Using depth formula: g′ = g(1−d/R); at d=R (centre): g′ = g(1−1) = 0. The shell theorem explains this: at the centre, all gravitational contributions from surrounding mass cancel by symmetry. Maximum g is at the surface (d=0 from the surface).

mistakes.label: Claiming weightlessness at centre implies no gravitational mass nearby:
mistakes.description: The Earth's entire mass surrounds you at the centre but the net gravitational field is zero due to symmetrical cancellation. Weight W = mg′ = 0 at centre because g′=0, not because mass is absent. NEET assertion-reason: 'A body becomes weightless at the centre — Reason: As distance from centre decreases, g increases' — WRONG reason. Correct reason: g at centre = 0 due to symmetric cancellation.

topics.example: Body of weight 500 N on Earth surface. Weight at depth d=R/2: g′ = g(1−1/2) = g/2; W′ = 250 N. Weight at depth d=R (centre): g′ = 0; W′ = 0. Variation: surface=max, decreasing below surface, zero at centre, also decreasing above surface as 1/r². NEET MCQ: weight vs depth graph should show linear decrease from surface to centre — zero at centre, not a maximum.
topics.trap_framing: NEET assertion — 'A body becomes weightless at the centre of Earth. Reason: As the distance from the centre of earth decreases, acceleration due to gravity increases.' Assertion is TRUE. Reason is FALSE (g decreases with depth, not increases). Trap: student selects A (both true and reason explains assertion) instead of C (assertion true, reason false).

trap_question.stem: At which of the following locations is the acceleration due to gravity maximum?
options: At height R above Earth's surface
trap_question.trick: g is maximum at the surface. At poles g is slightly larger than at equator because Earth is oblate (poles are closer to centre) and no centrifugal reduction. Option C (at poles) is the maximum among these choices. Option A (centre) has g=0. Option D (height R) has g=g/4. Between equator and poles, poles win. Correct: C.

topics.quick_rule: g profile: increases from centre (g=0) to surface (g=max), then decreases above surface as 1/r². Maximum g is at the Earth's SURFACE. g=0 ONLY at the centre (by symmetry). Weightlessness at centre: g=0, not 'infinite symmetrical pull'.

grounding_summary: Pages 404-415 (Gravitation MCQ section): Multiple NEET/JEE past questions on escape velocity KE = ½mv_e² = GMm/R, PE at planet surface = −GMm/R = −½mv_e², orbit energy relations KE = GMm/2r, binding energy, energy to move between orbits GMm(1/r₁−1/r₂)/2.

Pages 420-425 (Kepler's laws MCQ): Kepler 3rd noted as T₁²/T₂² = r₁³/r₂³; frequency form n₁²d₁³ = n₂²d₂³; ratio questions on period for ratio of distances 1.38 giving T ratio = 1.38^(3/2); assertion that Copernicus first proposed heliocentric model.

Pages 430 (Orbital speed MCQ): Orbital speed comparison via v ∝ 1/√r with 0.5% increase for 1% decrease in radius; escape from orbit KE = mV², relation v_e = √2 × v_o; geostationary orbit characteristics INSAT group.

Pages 435-440 (Kepler critical thinking + Assertion-Reason): Critical MCQ on areal velocity dA/dt ∝ ωr²; KE comparison at perihelion/aphelion K_C > K_B > K_A; Assertion-Reason set confirming: geostationary T=24h, satellite in ellipse orbital plane through Earth's centre (angular momentum), planet faster near Sun (Kepler 2nd), weightlessness at centre g=0.

Pages 445-450 (Solutions to g variation): Solved: g′=g/100 at h=9R; g′ at depth R/2 is g/2 with 500N → 250N; ratio g_altitude/g_depth for same distance (altitude 2% decrease = twice depth 1% for same h=R/100); satellite synchronous orbit r=42.08×10⁶m derived from T²=4π²r³/GM; orbital radius r=6.6R_E for geostationary.

Pages 450-460 (Satellite energy/orbital solutions): Centripetal force on cosmonaut at h=630km computed as 560N; orbital speed comparison rA/rB=1/4 gives v_A/v_B=1/2; energy to go from orbit 5R to infinity = 5GMm/(6R); angular momentum conservation v₁r₁=v₂r₂; escape from orbit KE = mV².
grounding_excerpt: Acceleration due to gravity at a height h above the earth's surface is g' = GM/(R + h)²
Assertion: A planet moves faster, when it is closer to the sun in its orbit and vice versa.
The square of time period of revolution of a satellite is directly proportional to cube of its orbital radius.
Assertion: A body becomes weightless at the centre of earth. Reason: As the distance from centre of earth decreases, acceleration due to gravity increases.
The time period of a geostationary satellite is 24 hours.
If the orbit radius is decreased by 1%, its speed will increase by 0.5%
Gravitational potential of earth at every place on it is negative. Reason: Every body on earth is bound by the attraction of earth.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I Chapter 8: Gravitation
sources.why_used: Primary authoritative syllabus reference for all NEET Gravitation topics. Verified Newton's law, g derivation, both variation formulas, escape velocity, orbital velocity and Kepler's laws against OCR content.

sources.title: HyperPhysics — Orbital Mechanics and Gravitation
sources.why_used: Cross-checked orbital velocity formula v_o=√(GM/r), escape velocity expression, and energy relationships for circular orbits including the KE=|E_total| and PE=2E_total relations.

sources.title: Khan Academy — Newton's Law of Gravitation and Satellite Motion
sources.why_used: Verified Kepler's laws, areal velocity as angular momentum consequence, geostationary orbit parameters, and escape velocity independence from projectile mass.

key_facts: F = Gm₁m₂/r²; G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻². g = GM/R² = 9.8 m/s² at Earth's surface
Above surface: g′ = g/(1+h/R)² ≈ g(1−2h/R) for h<<R. Below surface: g′ = g(1−d/R). g=0 at centre. g_max at surface
Gravitational PE: U = −GMm/r (always negative, zero at infinity). More negative = more tightly bound
Escape velocity: v_e = √(2gR) = √(2GM/R) ≈ 11.2 km/s. Independent of projectile mass and launch direction
Orbital velocity: v_o = √(GM/r) = R√(g/r) — decreases with r. v_e = √2 × v_o at same radius
Time period: T = 2π√(r³/GM) — increases with r. T² ∝ r³ (Kepler 3rd). Geostationary: T=24h, h≈36000km
Satellite energy: KE = GMm/2r; PE = −GMm/r; E_total = −GMm/2r. Binding energy = GMm/2r = KE
Kepler 2nd: dA/dt = L/2m = constant; fastest at perihelion, slowest at aphelion (angular momentum conserved)
Kepler 3rd: T₁²/T₂² = r₁³/r₂³. In frequency: n²d³ = constant for all planets around same star

review.notes: All four topics grounded in OCR pages 404-460. g variation formulas, escape velocity, orbital mechanics, and Kepler laws cross-checked against NCERT, HyperPhysics, and Khan Academy. Four high-frequency NEET trap topics covered. Content stays within NEET Gravitation syllabus boundary. No beyond-syllabus content (e.g., no general relativity).
